title: The Risks of Forcing Regime Change in Cuba
source: The Guardian - The Economist (Web Edition) 30 May 2026
content:
Following an operation in Venezuela, Donald Trump boldly declared that Cuba was next in line for systemic regime change. As the island suffers crippling blackouts, Trump has urged Cuba's communist rulers to make a deal before it is too late. The ongoing oil embargo has made everyday Cubans incredibly miserable and has forced the regime to the negotiating table. Consequently, a few political prisoners have been freed, and a flurry of minor economic reforms have been announced. However, the American administration remains focused on achieving total regime change rather than settling for incremental progress.

Military force is currently being weighed as an option to forcibly remove the entrenched communist leadership. Surveillance drones have been buzzing over Cuba, and an American nuclear-powered aircraft carrier recently arrived in the Caribbean. A swift, illegal incursion to decapitate the regime might succeed initially, but it carries immense long-term risks. Cuba's dictatorship is deeply ideological, making it far more entrenched than the leadership previously ousted in Venezuela. Furthermore, America’s track record of nation-building in the face of guerrilla insurgencies is notably poor, suggesting a full-scale invasion would be disastrous.

Instead of military action, pressing for a gradual transition appears to be the least bad option available. America could offer humanitarian aid and allow more oil to flow in exchange for the release of prisoners and democratic reforms. Providing free satellite internet access to all Cubans could also help create the necessary space for political opposition to emerge. Genuine economic opening would probably have to precede any substantial political transformation, which could take a very long time. If the administration negotiates shrewdly, it could profoundly help the Cuban people, but resorting to armed force will likely make things much worse.
